With the introduction of VAR to the Champions League next season, the Premier League is looking more backwards than ever.

Their bizarre decision to not go with the technology has already cost teams dearly.


As the Manchester Evening News report, the table would be very different after just six games with the introduction of VAR.

But the Merseysiders might be glad, in retrospect, that their advocacy of the video refs (VAR) was resisted by two-thirds of the clubs – including Manchester United and Chelsea .

Because if they HAD got their way, the league table would have a different look going into this weekend’s fixtures – with City clear at the top.

A meeting of all 20 clubs in April decided, by a clear majority, that VAR needed further trials before being introduced into the league.

“I think Manchester City agreed to it but the other top teams, United and Chelsea I think, they didn't want it,” Guardiola said.

“It's okay, it will happen though. Not just here in England, all around the world, the referees must be helped. Today the football is quicker, it's faster.”

Liverpool indicated before the meeting that they would also be voting in favour, with their chief executive Peter Moore saying “technology belongs in the game”.

But United and Chelsea joined with other clubs – known to include Huddersfield , Crystal Palace and Stoke – to chuck out the proposal.

Liverpool boast the only 100 per cent record in the top flight, with City and Chelsea two points behind.

But while Jurgen Klopp’s team has benefited from the lack of the controversial VAR system, City have been denied their own 100 per cent record by the lack of it.

With the introduction of VAR to the Champions League next season, the Premier League is looking more backwards than ever.

Their bizarre decision to not go with the technology has already cost teams dearly.


As the Manchester Evening News report, the table would be very different after just six games with the introduction of VAR.

But the Merseysiders might be glad, in retrospect, that their advocacy of the video refs (VAR) was resisted by two-thirds of the clubs – including Manchester United and Chelsea .

Because if they HAD got their way, the league table would have a different look going into this weekend’s fixtures – with City clear at the top.

A meeting of all 20 clubs in April decided, by a clear majority, that VAR needed further trials before being introduced into the league.

“I think Manchester City agreed to it but the other top teams, United and Chelsea I think, they didn't want it,” Guardiola said.

“It's okay, it will happen though. Not just here in England, all around the world, the referees must be helped. Today the football is quicker, it's faster.”

Liverpool indicated before the meeting that they would also be voting in favour, with their chief executive Peter Moore saying “technology belongs in the game”.

But United and Chelsea joined with other clubs – known to include Huddersfield , Crystal Palace and Stoke – to chuck out the proposal.

Liverpool boast the only 100 per cent record in the top flight, with City and Chelsea two points behind.

But while Jurgen Klopp’s team has benefited from the lack of the controversial VAR system, City have been denied their own 100 per cent record by the lack of it.
